1.synthesis:
Psalm 85 is a communal lament-prayer that moves from the memory of past restoration to the petition for present renewal. The opening confession of faith in YHWH's prior acts — 'LORD, you were favorable to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob. You forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered all their sin. You withdrew all your wrath; you turned from your hot anger' (vv. 1–3) — establishes the theological ground for the present petition. YHWH has already acted in precisely these ways; the prayer is for the repetition of what has already been done. The three verbs of divine forgiveness — 'you forgave,' 'you covered,' 'you withdrew' — correspond to three dimensions of sin's reality: its guilt (forgiven), its moral stain (covered), and its consequence in divine wrath (withdrawn).

The petition of vv. 4–7 addresses YHWH as 'God of our salvation' and asks for the reversal of what appears to be renewed divine anger: 'Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations? Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you? Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation' (vv. 4–7). The revival petition — 'Will you not revive us again?' (hălōʾ-tāšûb tĕḥayyēnû) — is the heart of the lament: the community is spiritually depleted and seeks the life that only YHWH can restore. The appeal to ḥesed (steadfast love) in v. 7 frames the petition within covenant categories: YHWH's hesed is the basis on which any revival can be sought.
1.historical_context: The psalm most likely reflects the post-exilic community's experience: YHWH has already restored the fortunes (vv. 1–3 — the return from Babylon), but the expected fullness of restoration has not materialized. The political and economic difficulties of the restoration community create the gap between what YHWH has done (returned them) and what they hoped he would do (full eschatological renewal). The 'revive us again' petition is not for initial salvation but for the renewal of a community that has experienced some deliverance but still waits for more.
1.literary_structure: The first section establishes the psalm's structural logic: past divine acts (vv. 1–3) create the basis for present petition (vv. 4–7). This is the typological prayer movement: what YHWH has done provides both the vocabulary and the confidence for what the community asks him to do again. The three 'will you?' questions of vv. 5–6 — Will you be angry forever? Will you prolong anger? Will you not revive us? — create an urgency that drives toward the direct petition of v. 7.
1.theological_application: The 'God of our salvation' address of v. 4 is the covenant claim that grounds all petition: YHWH's identity as the saving God makes the appeal for salvation appropriate. The request to 'revive us' — to give life that the community does not have from within itself — is the acknowledgment that spiritual renewal is entirely a work of divine initiative, not human effort. The Reformation insight that grace precedes and enables all human response to God is rooted precisely in petitions like this: the community cannot make itself alive; it can only ask the one who gives life.
1.christology: The triple forgiveness declaration of vv. 2–3 — iniquity forgiven, sin covered, wrath withdrawn — anticipates the NT's multi-dimensional description of what Christ accomplished: 'In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace' (Eph 1:7). The 'salvation' of v. 7 — yēšaʿ, the same root as Yeshua/Jesus — is the name for what the psalm's God provides. The 'God of our salvation' is the Father who saves through the Son whose name is salvation.

8.synthesis:
The second section of Psalm 85 is one of the Psalter's most beautiful convergences of covenant attributes, narrating a prophetic oracle of divine peace. The psalmist adopts the posture of the prophet — 'Let me hear what God the LORD will speak, for he will speak peace to his people, to his saints; but let them not turn back to folly' (v. 8) — and then announces what he hears. The listening posture itself is significant: the psalmist positions himself as the receptor of divine speech, the one who attends to what YHWH is about to say rather than continuing to speak himself.

The convergence poetry of vv. 10–11 is among the OT's most celebrated: 'Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss each other. Faithfulness springs up from the ground, and righteousness looks down from the sky.' The four covenant attributes — ḥesed (steadfast love), ʾemet (faithfulness/truth), ṣedāqâ (righteousness), šālôm (peace) — are personified as partners in an embrace. These four attributes, which in human experience often seem in tension (justice and mercy, peace and righteousness), are here portrayed as fundamentally united: they meet, kiss, and are found together. This is the eschatological vision of restored creation — not a world in which mercy overcomes justice or peace overrides righteousness, but a world in which all four coexist in harmonious fullness.

The culminating vision of vv. 12–13 — 'Yes, the LORD will give what is good, and our land will yield its increase. Righteousness will go before him and make his footsteps a way' — integrates the cosmic and the agricultural: the convergence of covenant attributes produces not only spiritual harmony but material fruitfulness. Ṣedāqâ (righteousness) goes before YHWH as his herald, preparing the way, and his coming produces the shalom that makes the land yield its increase.
8.historical_context: The prophetic oracle form of the second section — 'let me hear what God the LORD will speak' (v. 8) — places the psalmist in the tradition of Habakkuk ('I will take my stand at my watchpost and station myself on the tower, and look out to see what he will say to me,' Hab 2:1) and Isaiah's servant who 'listen[s] morning by morning' (Isa 50:4). The four-attributes convergence (v. 10) draws on the Exodus 34:6 tradition where ḥesed and ʾemet appear together as the self-declaration of YHWH's character, now expanded to include ṣedāqâ and šālôm.
8.literary_structure: The section's structure moves from prophetic posture (v. 8) → the content of the oracle (vv. 9–13). Within the oracle, vv. 10–11 present the poetic heart — the four-attribute convergence — while vv. 12–13 ground it in material and directional reality: YHWH will give what is good (v. 12), and righteousness prepares his path (v. 13). The movement from cosmic poetry to concrete reality is deliberate: the convergence of attributes is not merely an abstract theological vision but the ground of material blessing and historical divine action.
8.theological_application: The meeting of ḥesed and ʾemet, ṣedāqâ and šālôm (v. 10) is the theological resolution of the apparent tension between divine justice and divine mercy that runs through the entire canon. They do not kiss because one has overridden the other, but because in the eschatological vision of YHWH's coming, both are fully expressed and mutually reinforcing. The atonement is the site where this meeting takes place in history: 'God put forward [Christ] as a propitiation by his blood... to show God's righteousness... so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus' (Rom 3:25–26).
8.christology: The four-attribute convergence of v. 10 — where ḥesed, ʾemet, ṣedāqâ, and šālôm all meet and embrace — is fulfilled in Christ: John 1:14 identifies him as 'full of grace (ḥesed) and truth (ʾemet)'; Romans 1:17 announces that in the gospel 'the righteousness (ṣedāqâ) of God is revealed'; and Ephesians 2:14 declares 'he himself is our peace (šālôm).' All four covenant attributes converge in the person of Jesus Christ, who is their embodied meeting point. The 'righteousness [that] goes before him' (v. 13) is the herald of the coming King whose own righteousness is the path along which his people walk.
